items.question: Who uses Ridge and Lasso in practice?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers fitting tabular regression—finance, healthcare, marketing, and science teams use them as stable, interpretable baselines and for feature selection when p is large or predictors correlate.

items.question: What is the difference between Ridge and Lasso objectives?
items.answer: Both minimize squared error plus a penalty: Ridge adds λ||β||₂² (sum of squared coefficients); Lasso adds λ||β||₁ (sum of absolute coefficients). Ridge shrinks smoothly; Lasso can zero coefficients.

items.question: When should you prefer Ridge over Lasso?
items.answer: When predictors are highly correlated and you want all variables retained with moderated weights, or when a closed-form stable solution matters more than explicit sparsity.

items.question: Where does the regularization parameter appear in the Ridge solution?
items.answer: It adds λI to XᵀX in the closed form β̂ = (XᵀX + λI)⁻¹Xᵀy, improving invertibility and shrinking coefficients relative to OLS.

items.question: Why standardize features before Ridge or Lasso?
items.answer: Penalties sum over coefficient indices; without scaling, features with larger numeric range receive weaker effective regularization per unit of predictive effect, biasing which variables get shrunk or zeroed.

items.question: How do you choose the regularization strength λ?
items.answer: Search a log-spaced grid of λ values and pick the one with lowest cross-validated error (or within one standard error of the minimum); never tune λ by training loss alone.

items.question: Ridge (L2) vs Lasso (L1): shrinkage and sparsity?
items.answer: Ridge penalizes squared coefficients and typically keeps all predictors with small non-zero weights—good for correlated groups. Lasso penalizes absolute values and often sets some coefficients exactly to zero—good for sparse feature selection but may pick one of a correlated set.

items.question: Ridge vs OLS when XᵀX is nearly singular?
items.answer: OLS inverts XᵀX directly and can produce huge unstable coefficients under multicollinearity. Ridge adds λI, yielding a well-conditioned solve and smaller coefficient norms at the cost of intentional bias.
